session_hint: the S3 upload AccessDenied failure investigation
question: What was the root cause of the S3 upload failures?
turns: user
S3 uploads failing with AccessDenied errors specifically for the prefix s3://prod-assets/user-uploads/. Uploads to other prefixes like s3://prod-assets/static/ work fine.
user
Checked the S3 bucket policy first — it grants s3:PutObject on arn:aws:s3:::prod-assets/*. That should cover all prefixes including user-uploads/. Bucket policy looks permissive enough.
user
Investigated VPC endpoint routing: the EC2 instances don't use a VPC endpoint for S3, they go direct over the internet gateway. No routing misconfiguration. Ruled out.
user
Request timeout ruled out immediately — the error is an instant AccessDenied HTTP 403, not a timeout. The request reaches S3 and is evaluated.
user

Root cause found: the EC2 instance IAM role (app-server-role) has an explicit Deny statement added last Wednesday for s3:PutObject on arn:aws:s3:::prod-assets/user-uploads/*. An explicit IAM Deny always overrides any Allow, including the bucket policy Allow. The Deny was intended to restrict a different service but was accidentally attached to the wrong role.
user
This is a classic IAM evaluation order issue: explicit Deny wins over any Allow regardless of source. The bucket policy Allow is irrelevant while the role has an explicit Deny.
user
Fix: remove the explicit Deny from app-server-role. The Deny should have been added to the analytics-role instead.
user
Applied the IAM policy change. S3 uploads to user-uploads/ succeed immediately. Confirmed correct prefix isolation on analytics-role.
user
Bucket policy was correctly permissive throughout — it was never the issue. VPC endpoint was irrelevant.
user
Added IAM policy analysis step to our change-management checklist and integrated AWS IAM Access Analyzer into the CI pipeline to flag overly broad Deny statements. Incident closed.
